weeks.title: Introduction to Health Systems
objectives: Identify the major components of the U.S. health system
Describe how fragmentation affects patient access to care
Recognize the roles of key stakeholders in health care delivery
Connect health system structure to real patient experiences

activity.title: Mapping the Health System
activity.prompt:
The U.S. health system is often described as fragmented — multiple payers, providers, and levels of government all play a role.

Consider a patient recently diagnosed with Type 2 diabetes who is uninsured and lives in a rural area.
questions: What parts of the health system would this patient need to navigate to receive care?
What barriers might prevent them from accessing needed services?
Who are the key stakeholders (providers, payers, policymakers) involved in this patient's care?
How does the structure of the U.S. health system create these barriers?

projectConnection.intro: Think about the health issue you are considering for your Health Advocacy Project. This week's content on health systems gives you a foundation for understanding how that issue fits into the bigger picture.
prompts: What health issue are you thinking about exploring? Describe it in 1–2 sentences.
Which part of the health system is most involved in your issue — providers, payers, public health, or policy?
Who is the population most affected by this issue?
What barriers in the health system make this issue difficult to address?

nextStep.message: Now go to Health Issue Exploration in the Planning Notes. Use what you just thought about to start identifying the health policy issue you want to explore for your Health Advocacy Project.
nextStep.label: Go to Health Issue Exploration

weeks.title: Social Determinants of Health
objectives: Define the social determinants of health and their categories
Explain how conditions outside of health care shape health outcomes
Apply the SDOH framework to a community scenario
Identify upstream policy levers that address social determinants

activity.title: Applying the Social Determinants Framework
activity.prompt:
Social determinants of health (SDOH) include the conditions in which people are born, grow, live, work, and age.

Consider a low-income neighborhood with limited grocery stores, high unemployment, and poor public transportation.
questions: Which social determinants of health are most prominent in this scenario?
How might these determinants affect residents' health outcomes?
What upstream policy interventions could address these determinants?
Which government level (local, state, federal) would be best positioned to act?

projectConnection.intro: Social determinants often sit at the root of the health issue you are exploring. This week, think about the social conditions that contribute to your issue.
prompts: Which social determinants of health contribute to the issue you are exploring (e.g., income, housing, education, neighborhood)?
How do those social conditions affect the population you identified?
Does your issue affect certain communities more than others? Why?
Would a policy solution need to address social determinants, or just health care access?

nextStep.message: Go to Health Issue Exploration in the Planning Notes. Add or refine your notes on which population is affected by your health issue and why it matters.
nextStep.label: Go to Health Issue Exploration

weeks.title: Health Policy Basics
objectives: Describe the stages of the health policy process
Distinguish between different types of health policy (regulatory, financing, allocative)
Analyze arguments for and against a policy proposal
Identify the types of evidence that influence policy decisions

activity.title: Policy Analysis Exercise
activity.prompt: A state legislature is considering a bill that would expand Medicaid eligibility to cover adults up to 150% of the federal poverty level.
questions: Who would benefit from this policy change? Be specific about the population.
What arguments would supporters make in favor of this expansion?
What arguments would opponents make against this expansion?
What data or evidence would be most persuasive to policymakers on each side?

projectConnection.intro: Now that you understand how health policy works, start thinking about what kind of policy change could address your health issue.
prompts: What type of policy change would address your health issue — regulatory, financing, or allocative?
At what level of government would this policy change happen — local, state, or federal?
Who would support this policy change and why?
What is the strongest argument against the change, and how would you respond to it?

nextStep.message: Go to Article Research Notes in the Planning Notes. Begin organizing your notes from your first scholarly article on your health policy issue.
nextStep.label: Go to Article Research Notes

weeks.title: Healthcare Workforce
objectives: Describe the composition of the U.S. healthcare workforce
Explain the causes and effects of provider shortages
Identify policy strategies used to address workforce distribution
Connect workforce issues to access and population health outcomes

activity.title: Addressing Workforce Shortages
activity.prompt: A rural county is experiencing a shortage of behavioral health providers. Wait times for mental health appointments exceed 6 months. The nearest psychiatric hospital is 90 miles away.
questions: What healthcare professionals could address this shortage?
What barriers affect access to behavioral health services in rural areas?
How might workforce shortages affect population health in this county?
What policy solutions have been used in other states to address rural provider shortages?

projectConnection.intro: Workforce issues may be part of what drives your health issue or complicates the solution. Consider whether the availability of providers is a factor in your project.
prompts: Does your health issue involve a shortage or maldistribution of healthcare providers? If so, how?
Which types of healthcare workers are most involved in addressing your issue?
Are there workforce-related barriers that prevent the population you identified from getting care?
Could a workforce policy (training programs, loan forgiveness, scope-of-practice expansion) be part of your recommendation?

nextStep.message: Go to Article Research Notes and continue organizing your second scholarly article. Aim to have all three articles recorded before your Article Review assignment is due.
nextStep.label: Go to Article Research Notes

weeks.title: Health Disparities & Equity
objectives: Define health disparities and health equity
Identify populations most affected by persistent health disparities
Explain structural and systemic factors that contribute to disparities
Describe what a health equity-centered policy approach looks like

activity.title: Examining Health Disparities
activity.prompt: Black Americans experience significantly higher rates of hypertension, maternal mortality, and diabetes compared to white Americans. These disparities persist even when controlling for income and education.
questions: What factors beyond income might explain persistent racial health disparities?
How does structural racism contribute to health outcomes?
What role can health policy play in reducing disparities?
What would a health equity-centered policy approach look like for one of these conditions?

projectConnection.intro: Health disparities are often at the heart of why a health issue matters. This week, examine whether your issue disproportionately affects certain groups and what that means for your policy argument.
prompts: Does your health issue disproportionately affect a particular racial, ethnic, or socioeconomic group? What does the data show?
Are there structural or historical factors that explain why this group is more affected?
How does framing your issue as a health equity issue strengthen your policy argument?
What would a health equity-centered solution look like for your specific issue?

nextStep.message: Go to Article Research Notes and complete your notes for all three scholarly articles. When you are done, export your Article Research Notes PDF — you can use it as a reference while writing your Article Review assignment in Blackboard.
nextStep.label: Go to Article Research Notes

weeks.title: Health Advocacy in Practice
objectives: Define health advocacy and its role in the policy process
Identify key decision-makers and stakeholders in a policy campaign
Select appropriate advocacy tactics for a specific audience
Apply advocacy concepts to your own Health Advocacy Project

activity.title: Mapping Your Advocacy Strategy
activity.prompt: Think about the health policy issue you have selected for your Health Advocacy Project.
questions: Who are the key decision-makers with the power to change this policy?
Who are the stakeholders who would be affected by a policy change (supporters and opponents)?
What advocacy tactics would be most effective for reaching your target decision-maker?
What evidence or data is most persuasive for the audience you are targeting?

projectConnection.intro: This week connects directly to your project. Use this space to apply advocacy concepts to your specific issue and sharpen your strategy.
prompts: Who is the single most important decision-maker you need to persuade? (A legislator, agency head, school board, etc.)
What do you know about that decision-maker's priorities or concerns?
Which advocacy tactic would be most effective for your issue — a policy brief, public testimony, coalition letter, or media campaign?
What is the one message you want your decision-maker to walk away with?

nextStep.message: Go to Policy Advocacy Notes and start building your policy argument — what change you are recommending, who should act, and what evidence supports it.
nextStep.label: Go to Policy Advocacy Notes

weeks.title: Building Your Policy Argument
objectives: Construct a clear and specific policy recommendation
Support a policy argument with evidence from scholarly research
Anticipate and address counterarguments
Identify the appropriate decision-maker for your policy recommendation

activity.title: Strengthening Your Policy Recommendation
activity.prompt: A strong policy argument includes a clear problem statement, evidence of impact, a specific policy recommendation, identification of who should act, and a projected outcome.
questions: State your policy recommendation in one clear sentence.
What is the strongest piece of evidence supporting your recommendation?
Who is the specific decision-maker or body that should take action?
What is the most likely counterargument, and how would you respond to it?

projectConnection.intro: This is the week to sharpen and finalize your policy argument. Use these prompts to stress-test your recommendation before writing your Policy Letter.
prompts: Write your policy recommendation in one clear, specific sentence.
Which piece of evidence from your research is most persuasive? Why?
What outcome would you expect if the policy is implemented? Be specific.
What is the strongest counterargument, and what is your response?

nextStep.message: Go to Policy Advocacy Notes and finalize your argument. When ready, export your Policy Advocacy Notes PDF to use as a reference while writing your Policy Letter in Blackboard.
nextStep.label: Go to Policy Advocacy Notes

weeks.title: Presentations & Reflection
objectives: Organize an advocacy presentation with a clear structure
Deliver a compelling problem statement and call to action
Reflect on how the app supported your learning this semester
Prepare your App Reflection for submission in Blackboard

activity.title: Preparing Your Advocacy Presentation
activity.prompt: You are preparing to present your Health Advocacy Project. A strong advocacy presentation opens with the problem, presents evidence, delivers a clear recommendation, and closes with a call to action.
questions: What is the one-sentence problem statement that will open your presentation?
Which piece of evidence will you lead with and why?
What is your specific, actionable call to action?
What question do you anticipate from the audience, and how will you answer it?

projectConnection.intro: You have arrived at the final week. Use these prompts to pull your entire project together and prepare for your presentation.
prompts: How has your understanding of your health issue changed from Week 1 to now?
What is the most important thing you want your audience to know about your issue?
What is your call to action — what do you want people to do after hearing your presentation?
Looking back at all eight weeks, which week's content was most useful for your project and why?

nextStep.message: Go to Presentation Planning Notes to organize your slides and talking points. When ready, export your Presentation Planning PDF. Then complete the App Reflection — export it as a PDF and submit it in Blackboard.
nextStep.label: Go to Presentation Planning Notes

reflectionPrompts: How did the weekly activities help you apply the course readings to real-world health policy issues?
Did the planning note modules help you organize your thinking before writing your assignments? Which module was most useful?
Which stage of the Health Advocacy Project was easiest to develop using the app? Which was most difficult?
What parts of the app were confusing or not useful to you?
How could the app be improved for future HLTH 207 students?
